@everyone ANALYSIS 👆

@everyone 

On the close-up timeframe, price aggressively expanded higher, then pulled back in a controlled manner — a classic impulse → retrace sequence. This retracement is holding above prior structure and volume support, with price pressing back toward the key break level (~160–161).

This level now acts as the final inflection point:

•    Holding above it confirms demand dominance.
•    Acceptance above opens the path to retesting the prior high and beyond.
•    Failure would imply short-term rotation, not trend invalidation, unless deeper structure is lost.

This is where execution matters — entries are defined by confirmation, not emotion.

@everyone

CVX

Energy equities are reacting to an increasingly fragile global supply environment. Heightened geopolitical tension involving Iran and Venezuela raises the probability of supply disruptions, export constraints, or headline-driven volatility in crude markets.

Integrated majors like Chevron tend to benefit in these environments:

•    Strong balance sheets
•    Direct leverage to higher oil prices
•    Ability to monetize volatility without overextending risk

Even incremental instability tends to support higher oil pricing, which feeds directly into earnings strength and valuation support for large-cap energy names.

@everyone here’s an update on $SPY. It’s always good to check on it because as you track it you will get a good idea of which direction the overall market is going. 

We want to make sure that the direction of spy is in agreement with our plays (calls of puts)

So far $SPY closed above the green line with volume, which means we have a potential trend reversal in play. 

It is in the early stages of formation so do not assume this chart to be correct. We need more time to truly tell but this is a good start!

@everyone Either way, both TSLAs structure and OKLOs have (3) specific boundaries. Upper Band/Lower Band (Trade Zones) and Middle band (No-Trade Zone)

The plan is to watch OKLO, and see if it breaks above or below with volume into one of the tradable bands.

If so, we would have the opportunity to prepare for a dual trade plan. 

We first buy in the direction of where price action breaks, and then we would wait until the inflection point is reached (top part of upper band, or lower part of lower band) and we place a second trade targeting the opposite direction.

OKLO on watchlist 📌

@everyone NOW WE ZOOM IN 🔬

This trading quadrant inside the FIB Golden Zone (GZ) should show you exactly what I plan to do as price action develops. 

This should equip you all with everything you need to trade this properly.

Save these charts! 📊

@everyone asked me on Friday:

“Hey Stacks why are we in RBLX that company sucks!?” — Here’s my answer..

First off, it’s a starter position. But most importantly, (3) of my most reliable indicators have aligned when I look at $RBLX on the daily time frame (very strong for swings)

🔹 FIB Retracement: As you can see, the current price of RBLX is sitting at around $80/share which sits right at the bottom of the golden zone (GZ @ 0.382)

🔹 RSI: If you look at the bottom, price action/strength picked up right after RSI curled out of 30-40 range with a double bottom sequence in the past.. not saying the same thing will happen exactly BUT it’s pretty unusual to see more selling at these levels.

🔹 Anchored VWAP: This is the area where we should “feel” the most action because of the liquidity available. VWAP essentially reveals where the true average price sits and where most trading activity takes place.

So my conclusion is:

Its technically primed on the daily, it feels like the start of XMAS 🎅🏻 rally.. WTF do you think kids are gonna ask for this Christmas? HMM.. idk.. RBLX?!

Why not.. IM IN! 📈

@everyone BOTTOM LINE.

$TSLA is not random — it’s rotating inside a structure. Treat trend lines as entries, not conclusions, and let price dictate whether you’re trading calls or puts. 🙏🏼

@everyone LETS ZOOM IN.

On the lower timeframe, price is rotating cleanly between channel support and resistance:

🔹 Call bias when price pulls into the lower trend line + EMA support and momentum stabilizes

🔹 Put bias as price approaches the upper channel boundary, especially with RSI rolling or momentum compression

This creates a defined range for directional option plays, allowing traders to target the opposite side of the channel with controlled risk.

@everyone

$IREN — Price is pulling back into rising long-term support + the 200 EMA (yellow) after a strong parabolic advance. This zone has historically acted as trend-defining support.

Momentum is washed out (RSI ~low 30s), signaling sell-pressure exhaustion rather than trend failure. A bounce and reclaim of $42–$46 would confirm a higher low and open a rotation back toward the $55+ volume node. Loss of the 200 EMA weakens the setup.

Key levels:

Support: $32–$35 (200 EMA)
Pivot: $42–$46
Target: $55+

Same playbook as ALAB/OKLO pullback into structure & watching for continuation.

@everyone

$ALAB — Price is pulling back into rising support while holding above the 200 EMA (yellow) a key long-term trend filter. Structure suggests a bullish consolidation after a strong impulse leg.

RSI is resetting near the low-40s, relieving momentum without breaking trend. A reclaim of $155–$160 opens a move toward the $180+ volume node. Losing the 200 EMA would invalidate the setup short term.

Key levels:

Support: 200 EMA / ~$145
Pivot: $155–$160
Target: $180+

Trend intact. Watching for continuation.

@everyone

$OKLO — Price is holding the lower trendline of a descending channel, with momentum stretched and RSI near oversold (~30). 

This is typically where downside pressure begins to exhaust.

A reclaim of $90–$95 would signal a short-term trend shift and open a move toward the $100–$105 volume node. Failure to hold $75 keeps the channel intact and delays the bounce.

Key levels:

Support: $75
Pivot: $90–$95
Target: $100+

Clean setup. Patience required.
